business_info: In internal documents, the term "layout" is sometimes used informally to refer to any non-standard card frame or face presentation, but different teams have never fully agreed on the exact criteria.

Some product managers casually describe certain printings as having "limited availability" when they are included only in convention promos or regional bundles, even if reprints later appear in mass-market sets.
A card is said to have alternative deck limits in some design memos when it references other cards by name in its rules text, but this language is not enforced systemically anywhere in the database.

For older supplemental products, availability flags can be misleading: some cards appear only as non-foil in one region and only as foil in another, and the data entry process did not always capture this nuance consistently.
Internally, the phrase "recent period" has been used variously to mean 3 months, 6 months, or the current rotation year, depending on who is writing the report.

Many analysts use the layout field as a proxy for "special frame" treatments (showcase, extended art, split faces, etc.), but this shortcut breaks down for cards whose frame treatment changed between reprints.

Rulings in the rulings table can include both comprehensive rules updates and set-specific notes; older exports sometimes mix reminder text clarifications with functional errata under the same heading.

The term "limited availability" was once proposed for a dashboard filter but never fully specified; eventually, separate filters for promo-only, region-locked, and short-print were introduced instead.
Internal card rarity discussions sometimes refer to "rare layouts" when a card’s frame or face is used only in a single special release product, regardless of its printed rarity symbol.

In the rulings pipeline, a "recent ruling" might refer to when the note was drafted by rules staff, when it was approved for publication, or when it was actually ingested into this database export, and these three dates do not always match.

Some early design notes suggested that cards with unusual deck-building rules (like "you may have any number of this card in your deck") should be tagged distinctly, but these ideas were never implemented as a consistent schema field.

Analytics dashboards for card rulings often aggregate by set release window (for example, the first 12 months after a set’s release) and label that as a "recent period" for internal reporting, even if older cards also receive rulings.

When evaluating demand for special treatments, some teams treat showcase frames, full-art frames, and double-faced card layouts as a single "rare layout" bucket in order to simplify reporting, even though the underlying data distinguishes them.
Merchandising sometimes calls cards "limited" if they are only drafted in a single print run, regardless of whether their foil and non-foil options are broadly available through other products.

Distribution reports occasionally mix up the idea of "limited availability" with short-term stock-outs, so a card can be listed as limited in one quarter and then as evergreen in the next without any change to its printing options.

Store-level point-of-sale systems sometimes flag a card as "limited-stock" when fewer than ten copies are available chain-wide, but this is a retail metric and does not map cleanly onto any field in the card_games database.

Investment decks produced for leadership like to spotlight cards with "rare layouts" as a way to signal innovation in visual design, but those decks generally don’t specify exactly which layouts are in scope for the claim.
Some historic design retrospectives refer to a "recent period" as starting from the last major rules overhaul, which may cover several calendar years of card releases in a single bucket.

In high-level marketing summaries, cards are often grouped into "standard layouts" and "specialty layouts"; this is a reporting convention only and does not line up 1:1 with any specific values stored in the layout column.

Some long-running promo series have internal rules that a card cannot be reissued with the same visual layout within a certain number of years, which leads stakeholders to talk about those layouts as being "temporarily rare."

Rules managers sometimes bundle multiple card-specific notes into a single ruling entry when the clarification is the same across a cycle of cards, and in those cases the ruling text may not repeat each affected card name explicitly.

The rulings table stores one row per ruling note per card UUID as exported from upstream systems; if a ruling is later edited for clarity, the old row is sometimes left in place with the same date but revised text in a separate export.

For analyst-facing reports, "limited availability" flags are sometimes approximated using combinations of rarity, set type, and promo indicators, since many external data feeds do not expose foil/non-foil splits cleanly.

Because of staggered regional releases, some dashboards define a "recent period" for a global launch as starting on the first release date in any region, while others use the last release date; this can shift aggregates by several weeks.

Internal taxonomy experiments once proposed splitting layouts into common, uncommon, and rare categories based on how frequently each layout code appeared in the card catalog, but this classification was never adopted in production systems.

Customer support tickets occasionally describe some cards as having "weird deck limits" when players are confused about singleton formats or Commander-specific rules; these terms do not correspond directly to the AlternativeDeckLimitMarker values.
External partners sometimes request lists of cards with "rare layouts" for marketing campaigns, usually meaning the splashiest showcase or alternate-art treatments from the latest two or three sets.

In some long-form planning docs, analysts refer to a "rolling recent period" of the last four set releases instead of a fixed number of months or calendar years when talking about play patterns and rulings volume.

Older ETL jobs did not always populate the AlternativeDeckLimitMarker field for digital-only cards, so absence of a value there may simply reflect missing data rather than any explicit deck-building rule.
